title: Saltwater gargles and saline nasal care
summary: Saline is inexpensive and low-risk when prepared and used correctly. It can soothe a throat, moisturise the nose and mechanically clear mucus, but it is not a guaranteed antiviral treatment.
evidenceSnapshot: Nasal saline has possible symptom benefit with low-certainty evidence. Gargling is widely recommended for comfort but lacks strong evidence for shortening illness.

interventions.name: Saltwater gargle
interventions.finding: May temporarily soothe sore throat and loosen mucus.
interventions.practical: Mix about 1/2 teaspoon salt in a glass of warm water, gargle and spit.
interventions.cautions: Not for children unable to gargle safely.

interventions.name: Saline nasal spray
interventions.finding: Low-risk option for dryness, runny nose and congestion.
interventions.practical: Use according to the bottle; clean the nozzle.

interventions.name: Saline irrigation
interventions.finding: May improve acute nasal symptoms in some adults and children.
interventions.practical: Use a manufactured sachet/device where possible; only distilled, sterile, or previously boiled and cooled water.
interventions.cautions: Tap water can contain organisms safe to swallow but dangerous in the nose.

interventions.name: Hypertonic saline
interventions.finding: May reduce swelling slightly more than isotonic saline but can sting and irritate.
interventions.practical: Use a labelled product rather than improvising very salty mixtures.
interventions.cautions: Stop if significant burning or bleeding.

practicalSteps: Wash hands before preparing or using the device.
Boil tap water at a rolling boil for at least 1 minute, cool it, or use sterile/distilled water.
Clean and air-dry the device after each use.
Discard old homemade solution rather than storing it indefinitely.
avoidOrCare: Do not irrigate a completely blocked nose with force.
Seek advice after recent sinus/ear surgery or with immune compromise.
Stop if irrigation repeatedly causes ear pressure, bleeding or significant pain.

myths.claim: Australian tap water is automatically safe for neti pots.
myths.reality: Drinking-water standards do not make water sterile enough for nasal irrigation.

myths.claim: Saline washes viruses out and prevents infection with certainty.
myths.reality: It mechanically clears material and may ease symptoms, but evidence for preventing or shortening infection is limited.

redFlags: Severe headache, fever, vomiting, confusion or stiff neck after unsafe nasal rinsing
Persistent nosebleed or severe ear pain
unsettled: Best volume, tonicity and frequency for acute viral illness are not firmly established.
